pi_items.source: Found in task.json description and verifier.py DASHBOARD_NAME constant.
Found in verifier.py constants GRAPH_RAW_SPEED, GRAPH_REQUEST_RATE, and GRAPH_NORMALIZED.
Found explicitly in the task.json description ('You are the Incident Commander').

Contradicted by the data source. The task utilizes real datasets from the Numenta Anomaly Benchmark (NAB), specifically AWS CloudWatch metrics and Twin Cities car traffic speeds (Minnesota Department of Transportation) which are falsely portrayed as web traffic speeds. There is no evidence this represents an E-commerce company.
Found in export_result.sh which saves the file to this path, and verifier.py which reads from this path.

privileged_info_summary: The task operates on real-world datasets downloaded from the Numenta Anomaly Benchmark (NAB) GitHub repository. The scenario claims to represent an 'E-commerce' incident with 'web traffic speed sensors', but the data used for the speed sensors (speed_6005.csv and speed_7578.csv) is actually real car traffic speed data from the Minnesota Department of Transportation in the Twin Cities Metro area. The database and load balancer metrics come from NAB's realAWSCloudwatch dataset. The expected dashboard name is exactly 'UX Correlation Analysis' and must contain three specific graphs: 'Raw Speed', 'Request Rate', and 'Normalized System Behavior', which are verified by verifier.py by querying the Graphite database state at /tmp/normalized_ux_degradation_analysis_result.json.
